headline: April Vegetable Prices: Farmers Warn 30-50% Hikes as Fertiliser Costs Climb
context: One-third of global fertiliser shipments transit the Strait of Hormuz. After the late-February 2026 conflict, Hormuz traffic collapsed and World Bank flagged a sharp March fertiliser price spike. Malaysia's 14-28 March festive max-price scheme ended on 28 March.

cards.big: Vegetable farmers are warning of 30-50% price rises after Hari Raya. The Hormuz disruption is part of the story.
cards.sub: The 14-28 March 2026 festive maximum price scheme ended on 28 March. Domestic diesel, weather and supply-chain factors also feed into vegetable retail prices.

cards.big: Kpler observed 23 fertiliser-related vessels in the Gulf with Hormuz transit uncertain. World Bank flagged a sharp March 2026 price spike.
cards.sub: The Federation of Vegetable Farmers said input costs were expected to rise materially through April; raw-material price series vary by product.

cards.big: Lower-income households are more exposed to food price rises as a share of expenditure. DOSM shows food's larger weight in B40 budgets.
cards.sub: Distributional harm from food shocks can be obscured in aggregate CPI. Coping behaviour needs separate survey evidence.

cards.big: Indonesia stockpiles via Pupuk Indonesia; Vietnam's price law covers fertilisers; Thailand monitors fertiliser reserves.
cards.sub: Malaysia imports over 60% of mineral-fertiliser needs. No Vietnam-style stabilisation list or Thailand-style public-stock mechanism was found.

cards.big: The question is not whether external shocks are real. It is whether Malaysia has the stockpile, stabilisation framework, or farmgate-to-retail transparency to absorb them.
Hormuz raised the cost of input dependency. Malaysia lacks the buffers Indonesia, Vietnam and Thailand built; until that changes, shocks hit the wet market.
